options: The smallest deployable unit in Kubernetes, containing one or more tightly-coupled containers that share storage, network, and lifecycle
A cluster of Kubernetes master nodes that manage the system
A storage solution for persisting container data across nodes
A grouping of services that handle traffic load balancing
questions.explanation: Pods are the foundational unit wrapping containers with shared networking and storage. They're not clusters of masters (B), storage solutions (C), or load-balancing groups (D). Most Pods contain a single container, but multi-container Pods are used for sidecar patterns.

questions.q: What is the purpose of a Kubernetes Deployment?
options: To manage ReplicaSets and enable declarative updates, rolling updates, and rollback of Pod replicas
To provide persistent storage for stateful applications
To route external traffic to Pods
To monitor Pod health and restart failed containers
questions.explanation: Deployments manage replica counts and update strategies, allowing zero-downtime rolling updates and easy rollbacks. Persistent storage is PersistentVolume (B); traffic routing is Services (C); health monitoring is part of kubelet, not Deployments (D).

options: An abstraction that exposes Pods as a stable network endpoint with DNS names and load balancing
A long-running process that monitors cluster resource usage
A template for creating multiple identical Pods
A security policy that restricts traffic between Pods
questions.explanation: Services provide stable IPs and DNS names, abstracting away Pod ephemeralness and enabling service discovery. Resource monitors are separate (B); Pod templates are Deployments/StatefulSets (C); traffic restriction is NetworkPolicy (D).

questions.q: What is a ReplicaSet in Kubernetes?
options: A controller that ensures a specified number of identical Pod replicas are running, replacing failed ones
A backup copy of Kubernetes configuration files
A security group that allows Pods to communicate with each other
A volume that replicates data across multiple nodes
questions.explanation: ReplicaSets maintain replica counts and self-heal by respawning failed Pods. Configuration backups (B) are handled by version control; security is NetworkPolicy (C); data replication uses PersistentVolumes (D). Deployments manage ReplicaSets, not the reverse.

options: Adjusting the number of Pod replicas to meet demand, either manually or automatically based on metrics
Increasing the CPU and memory resources allocated to each Pod
Upgrading the Kubernetes cluster version
Moving Pods between different nodes in the cluster
questions.explanation: Scaling changes replica counts via horizontal scaling or Pod resource limits via vertical scaling; the most common is horizontal replica scaling. Resource allocation (B) is a separate parameter; cluster upgrades (C) and Pod migration (D) are different operations.

questions.q: What is the difference between a ClusterIP, NodePort, and LoadBalancer Service type?
options: ClusterIP is internal-only; NodePort exposes Pods on every node's port; LoadBalancer provisions an external load balancer
They differ in how data is encrypted inside the cluster
ClusterIP is faster than the other two types
They represent different versions of Kubernetes networking
questions.explanation: ClusterIP (default) enables internal communication; NodePort makes each node forward traffic; LoadBalancer provisions cloud load balancers for external access. Encryption (B) is orthogonal; speed doesn't differ (C); they're concurrent features, not versions (D).

options: Automatically scaling the number of Pod replicas based on observed metrics like CPU or memory usage
Automatically scaling the cluster by adding more worker nodes
Horizontally distributing a single Pod across multiple nodes
Automatically restarting Pods when they consume too much memory
questions.explanation: HPA adjusts replica counts based on metrics; cluster node scaling is different (B). Pods can't be split across nodes horizontally (C); automatic restarts are separate from HPA (D).

questions.q: Why would a team use a StatefulSet instead of a Deployment?
options: For applications requiring stable pod identities, ordered startup/shutdown, and persistent storage like databases or queues
For better security compared to Deployments
For faster scaling performance
For reducing storage requirements
questions.explanation: StatefulSets maintain stable DNS names and ordering for stateful apps; Deployments handle stateless workloads. Security (B), scaling speed (C), and storage reduction (D) are not differentiators—StatefulSets actually use more storage for persistent claims.

questions.q: What is a ConfigMap in Kubernetes?
options: A way to store non-sensitive configuration data as key-value pairs that Pods can consume as environment variables or files
A tool for backing up cluster configuration
A template for automatically generating Pods
A policy that limits which nodes Pods can run on
questions.explanation: ConfigMaps decouple configuration from Pod specifications, allowing easy updates without rebuilds. Backups (B) are separate; Pod generation is Deployments (C); node constraints are Affinity/Taints (D).

options: Automatically restarting crashed containers and replacing failed Pods to maintain desired state
Automatically fixing bugs in application code
Manually redeploying applications after failures
Restoring data from backups when volumes fail
questions.explanation: Kubernetes constantly monitors and replaces failed Pods/containers to match declared state. Bug fixes (B) are manual; manual redeployment (C) contradicts automation; backups (D) are separate recovery mechanisms.
